meta.siteTitle: El Paso Hub | Responsible Data Center Policy
meta.siteDescription: A community education and advocacy site focused on responsible data center policy, water stewardship, and transparent local civic engagement.
meta.ogTitle: El Paso Hub | Responsible Data Center Policy
meta.ogDescription: Plain-language resources and local civic information on data center impacts in El Paso.
meta.twitterDescription: Learn the local impact of data centers, find official contacts, and support responsible technology growth.

nav.siteTagline: We are all in this together

footer.mission: El Paso Hub supports responsible technology growth, transparent governance, and sustainable water policy.
footer.disclaimer: This website is created independently for the people of El Paso. This website is not affiliated with the City of El Paso, the County of El Paso, Legistar, Google News, Reddit, or any other organizations or brands referenced on this site.
footer.disclaimer2: The information on the site is provided in good faith and is believed to be verified and true. As information is rapidly changing, we enoucrage you to fact check information.

newsletter.heading: Stay Informed
newsletter.description: Get occasional updates on meetings, municipal projects, and community initiatives. We will not sell your information. Unsubscribe anytime once the list is live.
newsletter.firstNameError: Use letters A to Z only.
newsletter.lastNameError: Use letters A to Z only.
newsletter.phoneError: Enter all 10 digits or leave this field blank.
newsletter.consentFinePrint: By signing up, you agree to receive communications from El Paso Hub regarding community projects, local initiatives, and civic updates. We value your privacy and will never sell your data. Unsubscribe at any time.
newsletter.welcomeBody: Thanks for joining. We will send updates on meetings, municipal projects, and community initiatives.

home.atAGlanceBody: This platform provides the research and civic tools to help you influence municipal policy. Start with our plain language guide to understand industrial trade offs or jump to the government page to find your representatives and take action.

steps.title: Understand the basics
steps.body: What data centers are, pros and cons, and what matters in a desert border region.

steps.title: Follow what’s happening
steps.body: Headlines and curated links so you can see local angles as stories develop.

steps.title: Engage with local government
steps.body: Find your representatives, see documented positions, and catch public meetings when decisions are on the table.

quickFacts.title: Why neighbors are worried
quickFacts.description: Hyperscale Data Center concentrate water, power, and pollution questions in one place. These nine points are the short version. Click the cards to learn more.
quickFacts.readFullPage: Read the full Data Center page

desertHeat.label: Concern
desertHeat.title: Desert cooling penalty
desertHeat.description: Above 100°F, moving heat out of a building costs more energy and often more water than in mild climates—there is no free lunch in the Chihuahuan Desert.

waterAccounting.label: Concern
waterAccounting.title: Corporate water math vs local aquifers
waterAccounting.description: Restoration credits in one part of a basin do not physically refill the aquifer your neighborhood shares with a cooling system unless the permit says so.

regionalCluster.label: Concern
regionalCluster.title: A regional power sink
regionalCluster.description: Very large projects stack demand on the same grid and watershed as growth across the line in southern New Mexico—cumulative stress matters.

gasAndLoad.label: Concern
gasAndLoad.title: Gas generation and 24/7 load
gasAndLoad.description: Reporting on local utility filings describes proposed gas-fired capacity tied to a major data center; night-time compute still needs power when solar is offline.

airOzone.label: Concern
airOzone.title: Ozone and backup engines
airOzone.description: El Paso already faces tough ozone planning; adding NOx from new plants and frequent generator tests can make bad air days worse.

jobsVsSubsidies.label: Concern
jobsVsSubsidies.title: Few jobs per billion
jobsVsSubsidies.description: Regional reporting cites multibillion-dollar campuses (Meta’s El Paso project at about $10B in 2026 press) with only hundreds of net new permanent jobs. Clawbacks should be part of the deal.

waterImpact.title: Why water is a top concern
waterImpact.description: When cooling demand rises, planners must weigh household needs, drought cycles, and future growth.
waterImpact.body: This campaign pushes for efficiency standards, transparent reporting, and infrastructure choices that protect El Paso residents.

learn.metaDescription: Plain-language explainers about data centers, AI, water use, and how to read local infrastructure proposals.
learn.title: Learn
learn.subtitle:
This page walks through the basics of what data centers and AI actually are, why they are not inherently harmful, and where the real problems lie, with particular focus on communities in water-stressed regions like El Paso.

Every section includes sources you can open and verify.

dc.title: Data centers
dc.description: What they are, where they came from, and why they are not inherently harmful.
dc.whatP1: A data center is a building or campus of buildings filled with computers that store and process digital information for companies, governments, or online services. Think of it as a warehouse for computing: servers run 24/7, which means constant electricity and cooling. Cooling often involves water or large air systems, depending on design and climate.
dc.whatP2: Every time you send an email, stream a video, check the weather, or process a credit card transaction, a data center somewhere is doing the work behind the scenes.
dc.historyP1: Data centers are not new. The concept dates to the 1940s and 1950s, when room-sized mainframe computers needed dedicated, climate-controlled spaces. Through the 1970s and 1980s, corporate computer rooms grew alongside the minicomputer and early PC era.
dc.historyP2: The modern data center took shape in the 1990s with the rise of the internet: companies needed reliable, always-on server rooms to host websites and handle traffic. Colocation facilities appeared, letting multiple companies share the same building, power, and cooling.
dc.historyP3: By the 2010s, cloud computing concentrated workloads into massive hyperscale campuses operated by Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and others. Today these campuses can draw hundreds of megawatts, more power than a small city, and the pace of expansion is accelerating.
dc.badP1: No. Data centers enable services that modern society depends on. Hospitals use them for electronic health records and telemedicine. Emergency services rely on them for 911 dispatch routing. Weather modeling, genomics research, financial transaction processing, and government benefits systems all run on data center infrastructure.
dc.badP2: Modern life depends on data centers. The civic question is whether a specific facility’s design and location match the local water supply and power grid, with binding commitments rather than vague promises.
dc.badP3: A well-sited, efficiently cooled data center in a region with abundant water and renewable energy is a positive economic asset. The same building in a drought-prone desert with a strained grid is a different story entirely.

ai.title: AI & machine learning
ai.description: What AI actually means, how long it has been around, and why the technology itself is not the problem.
ai.whatP1: Artificial intelligence, in the way engineers use the term, refers to software that can recognize patterns, make predictions, or automate decisions under uncertainty, without being explicitly programmed for every scenario. Engineers mean statistics and optimization at scale. The popular image of a sentient robot misses how these systems actually work.
ai.whatP2: Machine learning (ML) is the most common approach: instead of hand-coding rules, you feed a system large amounts of data and let it find patterns. Deep learning is a subset of ML that uses layered neural networks, the technology behind image recognition, language translation, and tools like ChatGPT.
ai.historyTitle: A brief history of the field
ai.historyP1: AI is not a product of the 2020s. The field was formally named in 1956 at a Dartmouth College workshop, but the intellectual roots go back further: Alan Turing’s landmark 1950 paper “Computing Machinery and Intelligence” posed the question of whether machines could think.
ai.historyP2: From the 1960s through the 1980s, researchers pursued symbolic AI built on hand-crafted rules and expert systems. Results were promising but brittle. Starting in the 1990s, statistical machine learning gained traction as computing power grew and data became more available.
ai.historyP3: The deep learning revolution began around 2012 when neural networks dramatically outperformed older methods in image recognition. Since then, deep learning has expanded to natural language, protein folding, autonomous vehicles, and generative media. The field has been active for over 70 years. The recent hype cycle is just the latest chapter.
ai.everydayTitle: AI you already use every day
ai.everydayIntro: Long before anyone marketed “AI-powered” products, machine learning was quietly embedded in services most people use without thinking about it:
ai.everydayOutro: Many layers of the internet and the services built on it rely on statistical and ML methods. The internet itself predates modern ML, but today it depends on these systems for routing, security, content delivery, and more. AI is infrastructure you have been using for years. It only sounds novel when vendors put a fresh label on it.

realProblems.title: What we are actually worried about
realProblems.description: Data centers and AI are not the enemy. The problem is irresponsible siting, governance gaps, and corporate hype that inflates demand for rapid expansion.
realProblems.overviewDetailLead: This is a quick overview. For detailed issues visit the
realProblems.sitingP1: A data center that uses potable water for cooling in a region under chronic drought stress is a fundamentally different proposition than the same building in the Pacific Northwest. El Paso sits in the Chihuahuan Desert, depends on the Rio Grande and the Hueco Bolson aquifer (both under strain), and faces summer temperatures that push cooling demand to its peak.
realProblems.sitingP2: Ask whether this specific facility should consume these specific shared resources in this specific location, and under what enforceable terms. Whether data centers exist in general is already settled; the live debate is about this project and this place.
realProblems.hypeP1: The current wave of corporate AI marketing has created a gold-rush atmosphere. Companies are slapping “AI-powered” labels on trivial features such as search bars, email sorting, and photo filters, which either already used ML quietly or barely benefit from it. This marketing inflates the perceived need for new compute capacity.
realProblems.hypeP2: When every company claims it needs cutting-edge AI infrastructure, the aggregate demand projections balloon. Those inflated projections are then used to justify rapid data center expansion, often in regions where the sales pitch outpaces the actual local benefit or resource availability.
realProblems.govP1: Large infrastructure projects touch shared resources: water, electricity, land, roads, and the community’s quality of life. Environmental justice and critical infrastructure frameworks help communities understand risks and negotiate fair outcomes with accountability at the center.
realProblems.govP2: The gap today is that many municipalities approve data center developments using processes designed for warehouses or light industrial use, without requiring the water, power, and noise commitments that a 24/7 compute facility actually demands.

localLens.title: Desert Southwest context
localLens.description: Why the same facility that works fine in a wet climate can be a serious concern here.
localLens.waterP1: El Paso’s water comes primarily from the Rio Grande and the Hueco Bolson aquifer. Both face long-term depletion pressure from population growth, agriculture, and climate change. The city has invested heavily in conservation and reclaimed-water programs, and that progress can be undermined by a single large industrial user drawing potable water for cooling.
localLens.waterP2:
When reviewing a data center proposal, ask whether the facility will use potable or reclaimed water, what its peak daily draw will be, and what happens during drought restrictions.

These questions go beyond narrow technical detail. They are community survival questions.
localLens.gridP1: Summer temperatures in El Paso regularly exceed 100 °F. Cooling a building full of heat-generating servers in that climate is inherently more energy-intensive than in cooler regions where outside air can supplement mechanical cooling.
localLens.gridP2: Ask for expected megawatt demand, grid interconnection plans, backup generation fuel type, and how costs or outages could affect residential neighbors. A facility that promises 50 MW today may request 200 MW in phase two, after the initial approvals are locked in.

engage.title: How to read a proposal and speak up
engage.description: You do not need a technical background. Ask for numbers, timelines, and who enforces them.

questions.q: How much water will this facility use, and what kind?
questions.detail: Ask for annual gallons, peak daily use, whether it is potable or reclaimed, and what happens during drought restrictions.

questions.q: How much electricity will it draw?
questions.detail: Ask for megawatt capacity at build-out (not just phase one), grid interconnection details, and backup generation plans.
Promises in slide decks are not enforceable. Ask which numbers appear in the zoning conditions, permits, or water-use agreements.

questions.q: Who enforces the terms, and what happens if they are violated?
questions.detail: A commitment without an enforcement mechanism and penalties is a suggestion. Ask which agency monitors and what the consequences are.

questions.q: What is the net benefit to this community, specifically?
questions.detail: Jobs, tax revenue, and infrastructure improvements should be quantified and compared against resource costs and quality-of-life impacts.

engage.hearingsP1: Most large developments require public hearings before city council or the county commissioners court. You can attend in person, submit written comments during open comment periods, or both. Your voice is strongest when it references specific numbers (water draw, megawatts, jobs) rather than general opposition.
engage.hearingsP2: Check the city and county meeting agendas online. If a data center project is on the agenda, the staff report is usually posted several days before the hearing. Read it. Prepare two or three of the questions above and ask them on the record.

regionalEvidence.title: Lessons from other places
regionalEvidence.description: Short summaries with sources. For the full harm analysis, charts, and policy ideas, use the Data centers page.
regionalEvidence.fullAnalysisCta: Open the full Data centers impact page
regionalEvidence.waterTitle: Water: closed-loop language and real makeup
regionalEvidence.waterP1: Marketing may stress a closed fluid path next to servers, but heat still leaves the building. Large daily water permits are a signal that evaporation or makeup water is in the plan—not just pipe fill.
regionalEvidence.waterP2: Corporate “water positive” programs can help habitats elsewhere; they do not automatically protect the Hueco or Mesilla bolson water your neighbors drink unless agreements say so.
regionalEvidence.energyTitle: Energy: filings, gas proposals, and hourly reality
regionalEvidence.energyP1: News reporting on El Paso Electric filings describes modular gas generation and questions about who pays during and after any bridge period. Those details belong in open dockets, not slogans.
regionalEvidence.energyP2: Annual renewable matching is not the same as clean power every hour. Night-time load can still sit on fossil plants without massive storage.
regionalEvidence.airTitle: Air: ozone planning and backup engines
regionalEvidence.airP1: Research in dense data center markets shows generator fleets matter for NOx and particulates. El Paso’s ozone challenges make new combustion worth scrutinizing.
regionalEvidence.noiseTitle: Noise: cities tightening rules after the fact
regionalEvidence.noiseP1: Phoenix-area debates show retrofitting standards after saturation is painful. Measuring at the source property and capping decibels early prevents the Arizona pattern.
regionalEvidence.jobsP1: Press reporting places Meta’s El Paso campus near $10 billion in planned spend while citing on the order of 300 permanent roles as a company promise, not a contractual floor. That is still an extreme capital-per-job ratio if you take the headline at face value. Good Jobs First and similar groups document clawback policies so public money tracks outcomes.
regionalEvidence.mandatesP1: Reclaimed cooling water, intensity limits, public dashboards, clawbacks, and limits on running backup generators for profit are all on the menu—see the full page for a playbook.

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.description: Common terms you will see in data-center, energy, air-quality, and local incentive discussions.

entries.definition: Under Texas law, cities may use economic development programs (often called Chapter 380) to offer incentives such as grants or tax rebates tied to a project. Local contracts with a developer are often called the 380 agreement.
Contract terms that let a government recover part of an incentive if a company misses promised jobs, investment, or other targets.
A facility where multiple companies rent rack space and share power, cooling, and physical security rather than building their own building.

A measured pattern where land surface temperatures rise near large AI or hyperscale data centers after they operate, separate from ordinary urban heat. Studies use satellites to compare before and after.
A-weighted decibels measure how loud sound seems to human hearing. City noise codes often set dBA limits at a property line or fence.
A subset of ML using neural networks with many layers. Powers image recognition, language translation, and generative AI tools.

The fair treatment of all people regarding environmental laws and siting. It highlights when pollution or industrial burdens fall more heavily on some neighborhoods, often lower-income or historically marginalized.
Water vapor released by plants and soil. It cools the air naturally; paving and sparse tree cover reduce this cooling in cities.
The utility process to connect a large new electrical customer to transmission and distribution. It can require studies, upgrades, and public rate cases.
A harmful air pollutant (smog) formed when nitrogen oxides and other compounds react in sunlight and heat. It is not the same as the protective ozone layer high in the atmosphere.
A data center operated by a cloud giant (Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta) with capacity exceeding 5,000 servers and 10,000+ sq ft, often much larger.
Temperature of the ground, roofs, or other surfaces as measured from satellites or sensors. It can differ from air temperature in the shade and is used in heat and data-center impact research.
A subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data instead of following hand-written rules. Most modern AI applications are ML-based.
A unit of power equal to one million watts. A single large data center can draw 50–200 MW, comparable to tens of thousands of homes.
Pollutants from fuel combustion (including backup generators and power plants). They help form ground-level ozone and particle pollution.
An EPA designation for a region that does not meet a federal air quality standard until plans reduce pollution enough.
Fine particulate matter 2.5 microns or smaller. It can penetrate deep into lungs and is tracked in air quality indexes.
Potable water is treated to drinking standards. Reclaimed (or recycled) water is treated wastewater reused for industrial cooling, which is often the better fit for data centers.
The ratio of total facility energy to IT equipment energy. A PUE of 1.0 is perfect efficiency; most facilities land between 1.2 and 1.6. Lower is better.
A market instrument tied to renewable generation attributes. Buying RECs for annual matching does not by itself prove clean power every hour unless contracts require hourly alignment.

Training is the computationally intense phase where an AI model learns from data. Inference is using the trained model for everyday predictions. Per request, inference is much lighter, but aggregate volume is huge.
The tendency of built areas to stay warmer than nearby open land because of pavement, buildings, waste heat, and less plant cooling.
Water use divided by IT energy, often reported in liters per kilowatt-hour (L/kWh). It describes how much cooling water a site needs per unit of computing; lower usually means better efficiency.

dataCentersPage.metaDescription: Water, power, air, noise, and incentive risks from hyperscale data centers in the desert border region—and practical policies El Paso can adopt.
dataCentersPage.title: Data centers and regional impact
dataCentersPage.subtitle: This guide translates complex municipal contracts and environmental data into a clear resource. Understand how industrial cooling functions in extreme heat: the reality of grid demand: and the legal gaps in public oversight. Every section contains verifiable sources.
dataCentersPage.learnLinkBody: If terms like PUE, hyperscale, or RECs are new, the Learn page walks through definitions with citations.
dataCentersPage.learnLinkCta: Go to Learn — basics and regional snapshot
dataCentersPage.engageLinkBody: When utilities or councils post agendas, comments carry more weight when they cite docket numbers and measurable limits.

localGov.metaDescription: El Paso mayor and city council, plus El Paso County Commissioners Court — contacts, election timing, and stance notes.
localGov.title: Local government
localGov.subtitle: City offices (mayor and council) are listed separately from El Paso County (County Judge and commissioners). Contacts come from official city and county pages.
localGov.updateSuffix: when rosters or elections change. The file nests each seat under city or county with sitting and running lists.
localGov.noStances: Want to know their stance? Reach out to them directly and ask them to make a public online statement. Once their statement is online, message the link to Jonathan Zayan on Instagram at {instagramLink}.
localGov.vacantMessage: Vacant — add a sitting official in the bundle when known.
localGov.cityDescription: Mayor and eight city representatives (one per district). Phone numbers follow the city’s district line pattern (915) 212-0001 through 212-0008.
localGov.countyDescription: The Commissioners Court includes the County Judge and four commissioners (Precincts 1–4). County roles often touch roads, unincorporated areas, and the county budget alongside city decisions.
localGov.liveDataNote: Council Offices` roster verified via El Paso Legistar.
localGov.electionSeasonDescription: Shown only when activeElectionCycleIds is non-empty in the local government bundle.
localGov.findRepsDescription:
Your city and county representatives depend on where you live. The links below help you see which boundaries apply to your address.

Once you find your district, you can use the City Government and County Government sections to know who your representatives are.
localGov.findCityDistrictHint: City of El Paso interactive map (ArcGIS Experience).
localGov.findCountyPrecinctHint: El Paso County Elections commissioner precinct maps.

pledge.metaDescription: Resident and business pledge pathways for transparent, efficient data center policy in El Paso. Live signup arrives in a later phase.
pledge.title: Pledge
pledge.subtitle: This page is static for now: it explains what pledges will mean and reserves space for supporter counts once a backend is connected.
pledge.apiNote: Live submissions, moderation, and public name displays will ship with the API phase. For now, use this page to communicate expectations and collect interest informally.
pledge.residentBody: A simple commitment to share accurate information, attend public meetings when possible, and ask for source-backed answers on water and power impacts.
pledge.residentFuture: Future: optional public name display with consent + moderation queue.
pledge.businessBody: A pathway for local businesses and groups to signal support for efficiency standards, transparent reporting, and community accountability.
pledge.businessFuture: Future: badges for websites/social + efficiency promise language you approve.
pledge.newsletterDescription: Join the interest list while we connect a provider.

cityMeetings.metaDescription: Upcoming and recent City of El Paso public meetings, hearings, and board sessions pulled live from the Legistar calendar.
cityMeetings.title: City meetings
cityMeetings.subtitle: Live meeting data from the City of El Paso Legistar calendar. Meetings are sorted by date with upcoming events first.
cityMeetings.officialScheduleDisclaimer: This list follows the public Legistar API and may not show every scheduled meeting. For the official schedule, see the
cityMeetings.loadError: Unable to load meetings right now. Please try again later.
cityMeetings.participateTitle: How to speak at City Council meetings
cityMeetings.participateDescriptionOpen: Sign-up forms open at 12:00 p.m. on the Wednesday before the City Council meeting.
cityMeetings.participateDescriptionDeadline: The deadline to sign up is 9:00 a.m. on the day of the City Council meeting.
cityMeetings.signUpUnknown: The next City Council meeting is not in our feed. The sign-up form links may not be active, until they re-open.
cityMeetings.signUpUnknownWithHint: The next City Council meeting is not in our feed yet, but should be on {date} at {time}. The sign-up form links may not be active, until they re-open.
cityMeetings.signUpClosedBefore: Sign-up opens {opensDay} at 12:00 p.m. Mountain Time.
cityMeetings.signUpOpen: Sign-up is open for the meeting on {meetingDate}. Forms close at 9:00 a.m. on the day of the meeting.
cityMeetings.signUpClosedAfter: Sign-up for the City Council meeting on {meetingDate} has closed.
cityMeetings.callToPublicHint: Speak on a city-related item that is NOT on the agenda.
cityMeetings.speakOnItemHint: Speak on an item that IS listed on the meeting agenda.
cityMeetings.submitStatementHint: Submit a statement on an agenda item in writing instead of speaking.
cityMeetings.youtubeChannel: City of El Paso YouTube (live streams and meeting recordings)
cityMeetings.noItems: No agenda items available for this meeting.

takeAction.metaDescription: Ways to engage locally: city meetings, your representatives, voting, volunteer cleanups, verified sharing online, and amplifying trusted voices.
takeAction.heroLead: If you care enough to speak out or take action,
takeAction.waysDescription: Small actions add up. Choose what fits your time and comfort level.
takeAction.way1Before: Share your concerns and solutions at city hall. Use the
takeAction.way1After: page for schedules, agendas, and how to sign up to speak.
takeAction.way2Before: Tell your City Rep about your concerns and solutions. Find out who your City Rep is  and how to contact them on the
takeAction.way3Before: Our city government makes important local decisions. Vote to choose representatives who advocate for what matters to you.
takeAction.way3After: through the El Paso County Elections Department.
takeAction.way3RegisterAria: Register to vote (opens in new tab)
takeAction.way3DashboardAria: Check your voter status on My Voter Dashboard (opens in new tab)
takeAction.way4: Share important information online and in person with neighbors and friends after you have verified it is accurate.
takeAction.way5Before: Like and share content from people and organizations posting information about causes you care about (for example,
takeAction.handsOnDescription: Join neighbors-led cleanups and environmental groups working in El Paso.
takeAction.volunteerTrashMobDescription: Volunteer movement that encourages neighbors to get outside and pick up litter in their community.
takeAction.volunteerDesertRescueDescription: Empowering our communities to reclaim and preserve our deserts by eliminating illegal dumping.
takeAction.volunteerSunCityDescription: Weekly litter pickups and more to beautify El Paso and increase sustainability in our community.
takeAction.volunteerRescueRunnersDescription: Rescue Runners is a program initiated in El Paso, Texas, to recruit volunteers to help run and walk shelter dogs.

contribute.metaDescription: Submit tips for review or contribute improvements via GitHub.
contribute.title: Contribute
contribute.subtitle: Help keep this site accurate. Tips are reviewed before anything is published. Code and content improvements can also flow through GitHub.
contribute.tipBody: This form is a UI placeholder — wire it to email, Airtable, or an API route when you are ready. For now it helps neighbors understand the intended workflow.
contribute.githubBody: Open an issue for corrections or discussion, or propose edits directly if the repository is public.
contribute.modNote: When you enable submissions, add spam protection and a clear moderation policy aligned with your legal and community guidelines.

support.metaDescription: Keep this free community site active through optional tips or local business sponsorship.
support.title: Support this community resource
support.subtitle: This site is free for everyone to use. Community contributions help keep it active, updated, and independent.
support.optionalNote: Support is optional. If this site helps you stay informed, there are two ways to contribute.
support.tipBody: This site was made by Jonathan Zayan as a volunteer to help the community stay informed and engaged. I architected this website to be as cost effective and efficient as possible, but all websites will have 3rd party costs associated with them. If you find this site helpful, please consider supporting the ongoing costs of hosting and maintenance.
support.sponsorBody: Local businesses can sponsor the site and be featured as community partners supporting informed civic engagement in El Paso.
